modify-and-verify-tracked-files.label: Inspect and Record a Tracked File Change
modify-and-verify-tracked-files.summary: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.

sketch1.title: Inspect the Edit Before Selecting It

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Spot a Modified Tracked File
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

`volunteer.md` is committed, but its meeting time changed. Use short status to identify the tracked modification.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Use the compact repository report and read both status columns.

help.concept: A tracked file remains known to Git after it changes; status marks the difference from HEAD.
help.hint_1: Focus on the path named in the task and decide whether it is untracked, staged, or modified.
help.hint_2: Compare the final status with the exact state the prompt asks you to prove.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

`volunteer.md` is committed, but its meeting time changed. Use short status to identify the tracked modification.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-notes
mkdir -p release-notes
git -C release-notes init -q -b main
git -C release-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 4 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -A
git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..

cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 4 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -A
git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Inspect the Tracked Edit
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Read the unstaged diff for `volunteer.md` and confirm the old and new meeting times.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: A file diff explains the exact content change behind the status marker.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Read the unstaged diff for `volunteer.md` and confirm the old and new meeting times.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Stage the Verified Edit
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Stage `volunteer.md`, then inspect the staged name and staged patch.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: Staging a verified edit prepares exactly that version of the tracked file for the next snapshot.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git add volunteer.md
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- volunteer.md
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Stage `volunteer.md`, then inspect the staged name and staged patch.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Commit and Verify the Update
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

The meeting-time edit is staged. Commit it as `Update volunteer meeting time`, then verify the latest history entry and clean status.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: After commit, the updated tracked file becomes part of HEAD and the working tree can be clean again.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Update volunteer meeting time"
git log -1 --oneline
git status --short
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

The meeting-time edit is staged. Commit it as `Update volunteer meeting time`, then verify the latest history entry and clean status.

single-choice-1.title: Understanding Git Status Output
single-choice-1.prompt: What does the `git status` command show you?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.c: The state of the working directory and staging area

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of a Plain Diff
single-choice-2.prompt: What does a plain `git diff` show in this workflow?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: A plain diff compares the working tree with the staging area, so it shows unstaged changes.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: Unstaged line changes between the working tree and staging area
options.b: Only committed changes from every branch
options.d: The staged snapshot compared with the latest commit

multi-choice-1.title: Git Status Information
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which facts can `git status` report about the current repository? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Git Workflow Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of a typical Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Staging Changes
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to stage changes in `file.txt`: `git [blank1] file.txt`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Committing Changes
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Complete the command to commit changes with a message: `git commit -m [blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Detect a prepared edit to a tracked file, inspect it at both working-tree and staged boundaries, then record and verify the update.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

sketch0.title: Begin from a Known State
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A tracked file has a baseline in history, so Git can tell when its working copy changes. Begin by confirming what the repository looked like before the task, then read status after the prepared edit appears.

The right-column modification mark means the working file differs from the staging area. No new snapshot exists yet.

Starting from a known state prevents an old leftover change from being mistaken for today’s work.

For a tracked `schedule.md` with a prepared edit, begin with:

```bash
git status --short
```

A right-column `M` means the working copy changed while the staging area still matches the latest commit.

sketch1.title: Inspect the Edit Before Selecting It
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Narrow the diff to the file named in the task. Read the removed and added lines, and confirm that the change is both correct and complete.

A status mark proves that something changed; a diff proves what changed. Those are different levels of evidence.

If the patch contains extra work, fix the file before it reaches the staging area.

Then inspect only that tracked file:

```bash
git diff -- schedule.md
```

Read the removed and added lines before staging. The command proves the content of the change, not merely its existence.

sketch2.title: Review the Staged Version Too
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Once the working-tree patch is correct, stage the file and inspect the proposed snapshot. The staged name list confirms the boundary, while the staged patch confirms the content.

This second review matters because staging captures a version at a moment in time. A later edit would remain outside the proposed commit.

The two-review pattern makes the transition into history explicit.

Once the edit is correct, stage and review it at both levels:

```bash
git add schedule.md
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- schedule.md
```

The list verifies the selected path. The staged patch verifies the exact version that would enter the next commit.

sketch3.title: Record the Update and Recheck
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Commit the staged change with a message that describes the project result. Then inspect the newest history entry and short status.

The history should show the expected message. Status should show no remaining edit when the task is complete.

That final check proves both the snapshot and the working state, not merely that the commit command returned without an error.

Record the staged result and verify both history and state:

```bash
git commit -m "Update workshop time"
git log -1 --oneline
git status --short
```

The newest subject should match the result, and a quiet status should confirm that no part of the edit was left behind.
